text.title: Blast Radius Scoring Rubric Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable rules every PR scoring must satisfy. The scorer enforces them mechanically; reviewer prose cannot override the override categories.

rule.statement: Score every PR on three axes within 60 seconds: SERVICES (1=one module, 3=one service, 5=multi-service or shared lib), USERS (1=<1%, 3=≤10%, 5=>10% of MAU), REVERSIBILITY (1=feature flag, 3=redeploy, 5=irreversible migration or external side-effect). total = sum, range 3-15.

rationale.source: LinkedIn engineering — code review at scale

rule.rationale: A three-axis model is the smallest one that distinguishes blast types (scope vs reach vs cost-of-rollback). Single-axis "size" rubrics collapse these and mis-route trivial-but-irreversible PRs.

rule.statement: Route by total: 3-5 → light skim (≤3 min, focus on tests + obvious smells); 6-10 → standard review (≤15 min, run locally if possible); 11-15 → deep read (≤45 min, run locally, require second pair of eyes if available).
rule.rationale: Banded routing eliminates the "every PR is the same" cost and matches reviewer attention to actual risk.

rule.statement: Auto-bump to 11+ regardless of axis math when the diff touches: auth, payments, RBAC, secrets, migrations, deletion logic, money calculations, PII handling, cron schedules. These categories override the matrix.
rule.rationale: Most production-impacting PRs in the corpus were low on axis math but touched override categories. Overrides correct the matrix's blind spot.

rule.statement: Reversibility=5 (irreversible) PRs require a written rollback plan in the PR description — not a theoretical one. Missing plan blocks merge regardless of total score.
rule.rationale: Theoretical rollback ("we'll restore from backup") fails under time pressure. Pre-written plans force the team to confront irreversibility before the merge.

rule.statement: Record the score in the PR template field "Blast: S/U/R = X/Y/Z (total)" so the score is auditable post-incident. PRs without the field cannot be merged via the bot.
rule.rationale: An unrecorded score is not a score — it cannot be questioned, recalibrated, or used to compute base rates after an incident.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (PR has diff + description, surface inventory exists, score field present in template), execute the full rubric: axes → total → override → rollback-plan check → record. Do not skip the override check even on tiny diffs.
rule.rationale: "It's only one line" is the most-frequent post-incident justification; the override check costs zero seconds and catches the cases the matrix misses.

rule.statement: If ANY of the following hold, do NOT use this methodology: (a) reviewer merges own PRs without review — write tests instead, (b) every PR is identical scope (one service, one table) — score is constant, (c) one-line copy/CSS fix — skim and merge, (d) dedicated SRE staged-rollout gates already enforce the same routing.
rule.rationale: The rubric carries overhead per PR; below a minimum review volume / repo complexity the overhead exceeds the payoff.

example.title: Tailwind tweak with no override
example.code: PR #482: add Tailwind class to button label - Services=1, Users=5 (visible to all), Reversibility=1 (feature flag) - total=7 - override? no (no auth/data/infra path) - verdict: standard review, ~8 minutes

example.title: Password-reset email template
example.code: PR #483: change copy of password-reset email - Services=1, Users=5, Reversibility=3 - total=9 - override? yes (touches auth flow) - verdict: deep read, run locally, found missing CSRF nonce, blocked merge

example.title: Score-by-LOC fallacy
example.code: PR #999: 1-line config change "ENV=prod" - "it's one line, so light skim" - override? yes (cron + secrets) — missed - result: production outage 4h
